# Transcrição: 06-3-2-1

- **Fonte:** 06-3-2-1
- **Duração:** 12:32
- **Idioma:** en

## [00:00 - 03:04]

All right, I've given you a couple of ways already to find stories in your everyday life. Find stories from your present and from your past. I'm going to give you another one. This is a method that I use in workshops all the time. I actually use it with students, young and old. It's a game that I play at the dinner table with my family. It is a fantastic way to find stories from your past and bring them forward so that they can be useful in your present day. It's called 321. It's a game I invented that essentially operates on the premise that the finer the prompt, the more specific the prompt, the more likely you are to find something new. If I was to ask myself, let's say, to tell a story about love, that's sort of casting an enormous net into my life. The prompt of love doesn't really help me very much because it's not going to help me find something new. I'm probably going to immediately tell you one of the many stories I've told about love before and not find something that I have sort of forgotten from my past. So rather than casting a wide net, I like to think of this as a laser pointer. This game takes a prompt and forces you to take a very specific object and sort of carve your way through life with that object, like look back into your life with this object and find a story. So I'm going to do this right now. The 3 in 321 stands for the three prompts I'm going to receive. I'm going to use a website called perchance.org slash object. It's my favorite object generator on the internet, but there are lots of them. So I'm going to use one of those and it's going to give me three words that will serve as my prompts for the story I'm going to try to tell. The two stands for the amount of time I have to tell that story, two minutes or less. Now I say less because the story could just be 20 seconds long. I don't know what I'm going to get. And the one is that I have one minute to prepare the story. I'm trying to lower the stakes for storytellers in my workshops. I'm trying to get them to tell stories in a way that doesn't make them feel like there's a lot of pressure on them. We do this at the dinner table with my family all the time. ## [09:13 - 12:14]

the work that I'm doing in the business world. I don't know if these stories are going to be helpful to me, but I would not discount either one of them. I don't doubt that there's going to be a time when one of those stories will be useful to me, whether it's coaching a leader or trying to sell my product or helping a marketing team or working with an advertising team. I don't doubt that one of those stories might be helpful to me. I also don't judge them. That's important in 321 as well. Don't find a story and judge it to be useful or not useful. Judge it as a story. And if it means something to you, it can mean something to other people. We're just collecting stories. The storyteller with the most stories is the best storyteller, the most helpful storyteller, the most effective storyteller. We're collecting as many stories as we can. So that when we are in a room and we need a story, we have an enormous pile from which to choose. I'll also add that playing 321 is a really wonderful way to recover a past that deserves to be recovered. To find things in our past that we deserve to remember, that we should remember, that are often instructive to remember, and can really help us look upon our past and feel it's something important and meaningful and longer and more complete than we ever imagined it possible. It's wonderful for business. It's going to help you in a multitude of ways. I promise you. But it's also going to help you as a person. And the great thing about 321 is you don't need people to play it. I find myself in meetings quite often where I am in the meeting, but I know I don't need to be in the meeting, but someone else feels I need to be in the meeting. And when I'm in one of those meetings, I look down at my laptop or my phone, I bring up perchance.org slash object, I hit the number 3, click the button unique, and I get three prompts in the middle of the meeting. And while someone is blathering away at the front of the room or at the head of the conference table, I start to think about my past through the prompt of three objects. I think about those three objects, how they've intersected with my life, and I look for stories. Now, obviously, I can't tell those stories in those meetings, but I find them and I make lists of them. There's a reason why I have more stories than I have time to tell them. It's because I'm hunting all the time for stories. There's moments in your day when 321 is a wonderful way to fill some time that deserves to be filled. So whether you're playing this with friends or playing it at the dinner table with your family, or quite honestly what a lot of my clients do, play 321 at the beginning of a meeting to allow people to tell stories and connect and share more, to have a little bit of fun before you dig into the work you need to do. Whatever you're doing in terms of 321, we're looking for stories. We're finding stories. We're holding on to them.

## [12:14 - 12:32]

We're recording them, and then eventually we'll use them to help us, our business, our people, our customers get to know us, believe in us, and ultimately help us achieve the goals that we're seeking. So play 321. Truly, it's a wonderful way to find some stories.
